coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive self-referencing thermometry probe and advanced anti-counterfeiting based on the CDs/YVO4:Eu3+ composite materials
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Fang
Jia, Mochen
Sun, Zhen
Fu, Zuoling
coredata.dc:description: The multi-functional materials with temperature sensing and multi-color imaging have broad application prospects in our daily life. A novel temperature-sensing technique facilitates the self-referencing thermometry probe to possess high sensitive, excellent repeatability and superior temperature resolution. Herein, temperature dependences of the emission spectra in carbon dots (CDs)/YVO4:Eu3+ composite are investigated at temperatures ranging from 313 K to 448 K. Furthermore, the non-toxic polyacrylic acid (PAA) is also introduced to prepare a uniform anti-counterfeit ink, which can significantly improve the afterglow performance by further hardening the CDs. Particularly, CDs/YVO4:Eu3+/PAA composites exhibit multi-mode emission color tuning in advanced optical anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Composite materials
Multi-mode luminescence
Temperature-dependent
Fluorescence intensity ratio
Anti-counterfeiting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs (the inset: high-resolution TEM image of CDs). (b) Size distribution of CDs. (c) FT-IR spectra of CDs. (d) Typical TEM image of YVO4:10%Eu3+ powders. (e) XRD patterns of CDs and YVO4:10%Eu3+ samples, bars at button present the standard data of YVO4 (PDF no. 17–0341).

(a) The normalized UV–vis absorbance (black dashed line), emission (red line), and excitation (blue line) spectra of the CDs (the inset: water dispersion under natural light and UV lamp (365 nm)). (b) PL excitation (λem=618 nm) and DC emission spectra (λex=320 nm) of the YVO4:10%Eu3+. (c) Schematic illustration of the PL and afterglow emission mechanisms for CDs and energy transfer scheme in YVO4:Eu3+ phosphors. (d) Temperature-dependent characteristic emission spectra of YVO4:10%Eu3+ powders under 320 nm excitation (the inset shows the change of integral intensities of Eu3+). (e) Temperature dependent emission spectra of CDs. (f) Excitation spectra of CDs monitored at 430 nm emission and YVO4:Eu3+ monitored at 618 nm emission. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Temperature-dependent emission spectra of self-referencing thermometry probe (λex=340 nm). (b) The emission intensities of 430 nm and 618 nm at various temperatures (313 K–448 K). (c) Intensity ratio of Eu3+ and CDs emissions in the temperature range from 313 K to 448 K. (d) The temperature-dependent Sr variation of the self-referencing thermometry probe. (e) Five cycles of intensity ratio of I618/I430 (alternating between 313 K and 448 K). (f) CIE chromaticity coordinates of the self-referencing thermometry probe at different temperatures. The insets show photographs of powders under 340 nm excitation at 313 K and 448 K.

The photographs of the pattern, under the excitation of (a) daylight, (b) 254 nm UV lamp on, (c)365 nm UV lamp on, (d) both 254 nm and 365 nm on, (e) 365 nm off, respectively. (f) Afterglow emission images of the pattern after ceasing the UV lamp (365 nm) irradiation for 1 s to 10 s.
Maximum Sa and Sr values of several typical temperature sensing materials.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive self-referencing thermometry probe and advanced anti-counterfeiting based on the CDs/YVO4:Eu3+ composite materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Coherent Light and Atomic and Molecular Spectroscopy Laboratory, Key Laboratory of physics and Technology for Advanced Batteries, College of Physics, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Coherent Light and Atomic and Molecular Spectroscopy Laboratory
Key Laboratory of physics and Technology for Advanced Batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Coherent Light and Atomic and Molecular Spectroscopy Laboratory, Key Laboratory of physics and Technology for Advanced Batteries, College of Physics, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The multi-functional materials with temperature sensing and multi-color imaging have broad application prospects in our daily life. A novel temperature-sensing technique facilitates the self-referencing thermometry probe to possess high sensitive, excellent repeatability and superior temperature resolution. Herein, temperature dependences of the emission spectra in carbon dots (CDs)/YVO4:Eu3+ composite are investigated at temperatures ranging from 313 K to 448 K. Furthermore, the non-toxic polyacrylic acid (PAA) is also introduced to prepare a uniform anti-counterfeit ink, which can significantly improve the afterglow performance by further hardening the CDs. Particularly, CDs/YVO4:Eu3+/PAA composites exhibit multi-mode emission color tuning in advanced optical anti-counterfeiting.

ce:sections.ce:para: In recent years, the temperature-dependent optical properties of rare earth (RE) doped luminescent materials have been extensively studied for non-invasive and real-time temperature detection [1–3]. This non-contact thermometry overcomes the temperature detection of complex conditions, such as corrosive environments, in vivo and fast-moving objects, which can enable remote detection with high accuracy and spatial resolution [4]. Compared with the thermometry based on absolute luminescence intensity, bandwidth and lifetime, the fluorescence intensity ratio (FIR) technique of two emissions of lanthanide ions (Ln3+) has drawn much attention due to its independence of spectral losses and fluctuations of excitation density [5,6]. The temperature-dependent populations of thermally coupled energy levels (TCELs) is an important means of FIR technique, such as Er3+ [7], Tm3+ [8,9], Ho3+ [10], Nd3+ [11], Gd3+ [12]. However, this temperature measurement technology that relies on TCELs limits the improvement of the relative sensitivity Sr due to its inherent limitations [13]. In order to further improve the Sr, finding novel materials or novel temperature sensing strategies has become a common concern of researchers. So dual-mode optical systems with multiple emission centers are very popular now [14,15].

In addition, data security has become a global problem. The production and sale of fraudulent goods causing significant damage and serious security threats to customers, companies and the nation [16]. Recently, lanthanide doped nanoparticles, quantum dots and fluorescent dyes are widely used in information coding due to their excellent fluorescence efficiency and good printability [17,18]. However, traditional methods based on a single-mode fluorescence such as water marking, color showing and barcoding are not very precise, because these strategies are outdated and easy to copy [19]. In addition, organic phosphorescent materials are not suitable for long-term information storage due to their low thermal and light stability. Regular patterns of multicolor nano-taggants have unique advantages such as spatially resolvable color features under a characteristic excitation wavelength, but this technique usually requires complex fabrication processes [20,21]. To this end, it is necessary to obtain a simple synthesis scheme and cheap materials with multi-mode emission characteristics to combat complex counterfeiters. Optical materials with tunable emitting colors and long-lifetime emissions are treated as a promising candidate for optical anti-counterfeiting due to their advantages in constructing multiple-mode information encryption system and eliminating the background noise [22].

In this study, the ultra-long afterglow carbon dots (CDs) was prepared simply and quickly by one-step heating process (Fig. S1a) and YVO4:Eu3+ nanoparticles were synthesized by hydrothermal method (Fig. S1b). The morphologies of CDs were studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 a, the particles displayed a good dispersed quasi-spherical shape without aggregation. The inset of Fig. 1a demonstrated the high-resolution TEM of the lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the lattice spacing in the (100) planes of graphene [23,24]. 100 nanoparticles of CDs were randomly selected for calculation. As seen in Fig. 1b, the size distribution was between 2.6–5.8 nm with the average diameter of 3.8 nm. In Fig. 1c, the Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR) absorption bands of CDs at 2500–3500 cm−1 (including the peaks at 2923, 2824 and 2720 cm−1) could be attributed to the stretching vibrations of - OH, - NH2, - CH2 groups [22]. The absorption peak at around 1634 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of CO or bending vibration of CC [25,26]. The peaks at 1520, 1348, 1045, 925 cm−1 may be attributed to the vibrations of NH, CN, P–O, and P–N, respectively [27]. Finally, the emergence of peaks at 2352 and 2116 cm−1 revealed the presence of phosphate and amine salts [28]. The pronounced absorption peaks at 879, 704 and 487 cm−1 indicated the existence of P–O–P groups [29,30]. The typical TEM image of YVO4 was given in Fig. 1d and the average particle size range was about 30–40 nm with slight aggregation. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of CDs in Fig. 1e presented a broad peak centered around 2θ=22.5°, which was attributed to the (002) lattice spacing of highly disordered carbon atoms [31]. All diffraction peaks of YVO4: Eu3+ could be readily indexed as pure tetragonal phase of YVO4 (PDF no. 17–0341) without any impurities in the host structure.

In order to fully study the photophysical properties of the CDs, the normalized Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption, photoluminescence (PL) excitation and emission spectra were displayed in Fig. 2 a. Obvious absorption peaks of CDs at 280 and 340 nm were attributed to the π-π* transition of the conjugated CC band and n-π* transitions of CO or OCO functional groups, respectively [24,32]. It was worth mentioning that the absorption peak at 340 nm was consistent with the excitation spectra, which further confirmed that the fluorescence emission was caused by absorption at 340 nm. Therefore, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs were 365 and 430 nm respectively. The aqueous solution of CDs was almost transparent in the natural light environment. Under the irradiation of portable UV lamp (365 nm), CDs exhibited bright blue emission (shown in the inset of Fig. 2a). In this work, the excitation-dependent emission behavior of CDs was presented in Fig. S2. With the progressing from 300 to 420 nm of excitation wavelengths, the emission spectra of CDs decreased gradually with the redshift. The strongest PL peak appeared at 430 nm upon excitation at 360 nm, which was highly consistent with the excitation and PL spectra (Fig. 2a). In addition, the CDs powders showed the blue light emission by irradiating with ultraviolet light (365 nm). After the light source was turned off, a green afterglow light appeared and it can last about 5 s (Fig. S3). The emission spectra of Eu3+ with different contents were shown in Fig. S4, and the optimal doping concentration was determined to be 10%. PL excitation and emission spectra of YVO4:10%Eu3+ were displayed in Fig. 2b. The excitation spectra of YVO4:Eu3+ were composed of a broad charge transfer band of VO4 3− at 320 nm and two sharp lines of Eu3+ at 381 nm (7F0-5G4), 395 nm (7F0-5L6). The characteristic emission peaks of Eu3+ at 594 nm, 618 nm, 651 nm, and 703 nm can be also detected, which were attributed to the transitions of 5D0-7F1, 7F2, 7F3, and 7F4, respectively [33,34]. Energy transfer scheme of CDs and YVO4:Eu3+ was showed in Fig. 2c. The electron of CC band in carbon nucleus absorbed 365 nm photons from the ground state (S0) to the excited state (Sn), and the electrons in Sn fell into the lowest excited state S1 by internal conversion [26,35]. Some of the carbon nuclei radiated in the form of blue fluorescence, and some of them passed from S1 to Tn in intersystem crossing (ISC). Then the radiative transition from the lowest excited triplet state (T1) to the ground state (S0) emitted a corresponding green afterglow. In the YVO4 system, the VO4 3− charge transfer band has a higher energy level, which can effectively absorb high-energy ultraviolet light (320 nm). Partial energy absorbed by VO4 3 − can be transferred to 5G4, 5L6 and 5D3 of Eu3+ in a non-radiative form [36]. The non-radiative process relaxed inside the Eu3+ level to the 5D0 level, thereby realizing the red characteristic luminescence of 5D0-7F1, 7F2, 7F3, and 7F4, respectively. Temperature-dependent characteristic emission spectra of YVO4:1%Eu3+ powders under excitation at 320 nm were shown in Fig. S5. When the concentration of Eu3+ was 1%, the integrated intensity of Eu3+ increased first and then decreased significantly with increasing temperature (the inset of Fig. S5). In addition, when the concentration of Eu3+ was 5% (Fig. S6), the integrated intensity of Eu3+ increased first and then stabilized (the inset of Fig. S6). Furthermore, the emission intensity of Eu3+ in YVO4:10%Eu3+ showed an increasing trend (Fig. 2d) and the integral intensity increased almost linearly (the inset of Fig. 2d) with the increasing temperature. As the concentration of Eu3+ increased, the energy transfer efficiency from VO4 3− to Eu3+ was improved [36,37]. 5D0 level of Eu3+ can capture more energy in high concentration systems so that the emission of Eu3+ will not be significantly quenched during the heating process. As described in Fig. 2e, the emission intensity of CDs decreased significantly within the wide range of 313–448 K. According to previous reports [38,39] , the good thermosensitivity of CDs could be attributed the photoexcited electron relaxation such as nonradiative relaxation, radiative relaxation and thermally activated trapping in surface states. This property enabled CDs/YVO4:10%Eu3+ composite to be an excellent candidate for non-invasion self-referencing optical thermometer.

Next, a self-referencing thermometry probe was prepared by physically mixing the two powders of CDs and YVO4:10%Eu3+ at a ratio of 1: 1 (step ① in Fig. S1c). The CDs/YVO4:Eu3+ composite maintained the respective characteristic emission of Eu3+ and CDs (red of Eu3+ under 254 nm excitation, blue of CDs under 365 nm excitation, weak green afterglow of CDs after 365 nm off). As depicted in Fig. 2f, the luminescence of CDs and Eu3+ could be detected simultaneously under the excitation of 340 nm, which was chosen as the optimum excitation wavelength for self-referencing thermometry probe experiments. As shown in Fig. 3 a, temperature-dependent emission spectra of the self-referencing thermometry probe were observed. As the temperature increased from 313 K to 448 K, the broadband blue emission of CDs weakened quickly, while the luminescence of Eu3+ at 618 nm showed an increasing trend. The relationships of integrated intensities of the CDs (430 nm) and Eu3+:5D0-7F2 (618 nm) transitions were plotted in Fig. 3b, where the emission of CDs decreased significantly, while that of Eu3+ increased almost linearly at 313 K–448 K, suggesting a potential of the CDs/YVO4:Eu3+ composite in ratiometric optical thermometer. In this case, emission intensity ratio of Eu3+ and CDs can easily be used to calibrate the temperature without any additional calibration of initial emission intensity. Correspondingly, an exponential curve fitting on the fluorescence intensity ratio (R) of Eu3+ (618 nm) to CDs (430 nm) emissions in the temperature range from 313 K to 448 K was performed in Fig. 3c. The relationship between the R and temperature (T) was well approximated by following Arrhenius equation:
R = 57.68 exp
(
−
1915.66
T
)

According to the relative change of R with respect to the variation of T, the relative sensitivity Sr can be defined as follows:
S r
=
1 R
d R
d T
=
1915.66
T 2

Fig. 3d showed that the Sr reached maximum of 1.96% K −1 at 313 K. The Sr values of other materials previously reported were listed in Table 1 . By comparison, the excellent result of CDs/YVO4:Eu3+ composite was achieved with operating temperature range (313 K–448 K) in this work. Although the sensitivity Sr in this study was not the highest, this strategy provided a reference for developing high-performance optical thermometers compared to some conventional materials. As confirmed in Fig. 3e, after several cycles of experiments, this temperature-dependent R (I618/I430) was reversible and repeatable, indicating the promising application in the temperature sensing. In addition, the self-referencing thermometry probe also showed a significant emission color tuning based on the temperature-dependent emission of CDs and YVO4:Eu3+ (see Fig. 3f). The chromaticity coordinates shifted from A (0.2242, 0.2063) at 313 K to B (0.3538, 0.2553) at 448 K and the emission color displayed an evolution from blue to pink over temperatures between 313 K and 448 K (the inset of Fig. 3f). The result suggested that this temperature-dependent luminescence strategy has an application prospect in the field of anti-counterfeiting.

It is well-known that the room temperature anti-counterfeiting is more popular. Therefore, we fully dissolved the CDs and YVO4:Eu3+ at a ratio of 1: 1 in a crosslinking agent of polyacrylic acid (PAA) (step ② in Fig. S1 (c)), and quickly obtained a room-temperature anti-counterfeiting ink. Next, drew the security pattern with prepared ink of CDs/YVO4:Eu3+/PAA composites on the experimental weighing paper and dried it completely. We can't see any signs with the naked eye under ambient light (Fig. 4 a). Under the irradiation of a portable UV lamp (254 nm), the red patterns were observed (Fig. 4b). Because there was no absorption at 365 nm in YVO4:Eu3+, the blue emission from CDs was dominated under 365 nm UV light excitation (Fig. 4c), which was consistent with the excitation spectra of Fig. 2f. When we turned off the 365 nm UV lamp, the bright green afterglow was observed (Fig. 4e). Under excitation at 365 nm and 254 nm, the white security patterns appeared (Fig. 4d). Such four-mode imaging can be easily and quickly identified with the naked eye, and the light source was also easy to obtain. It was worth mentioning that the introduction of PAA solidified the CDs, which significantly enhanced the afterglow's luminescence and afterglow time compared to single CDs powders (Fig. 2e). This high-molecular polymer acted like a rigid matrix to restrain molecular vibration [47]. As a result, the quenching effect of oxygen and water molecules in the surrounding environment was reduced, and the triplet excitons could survive long time to achieve a long-afterglow green phosphorescence at room temperature.

In summary, an effective and facile strategy was described to fabricate multicolor dynamic luminescent materials through blending CDs and YVO4:Eu3+. Depending on the difference between the unique energy transfer modes inside the CDs and the charge transfer band of VO4 3−, CDs and YVO4:Eu3+ were cleverly mixed to make a high-sensitivity self-referencing thermometry probe (Sr = 1.96% K−1). In addition, the use of PAA allowed the CDs/YVO4:Eu3+ composite to be dispersed uniformly and helped to enhance the triplet emission, which exhibited unique red, blue, white and long-lifetime green emissions controllably. This concealed information was hard to be copied or pirated. All the results could provide some guidance for the rational design anti-counterfeiting materials based on temperature-dependent emission spectra.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.11874182), Science and Technology Project of the 13th Five-Year Plan of Jilin Provincial Department of Education (No.JJKH20190179KJ), Special funds for provincial industrial innovation in Jilin Province (No. 2018C043-4) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.scriptamat.2020.05.015.
